title: Went from a high review from a 1 star in a year.
text:
I loved this watch when I first got it. A few months in I had a couple of issues with accidentally resetting it because my wrist would press the middle button, but I adjusted and eventually an update came came down which changed that configuration so it didn’t happen anymore.

Now I’ve had it for just over a year. The app and the watch have been updated a few more times. The app is still not really easy to use or intuitive. It takes a minute to figure it out. What has me changing my rating is that now not only has the watch completely come disconnected from the app, it will not even recognize it anymore to pair it again AND the sleep tracking function (which is the main reason I wanted this watch) has literally disappeared. I checked the app, it stopped tracking my sleep a week ago. No notification to me this would be changing mind you. So I went to the help section (as you do), it said to update my watch version. Ok fine. I followed the instructions except it’s already up to date. I checked the watch itself to re-initiate the sleep tracking as the help guide suggested. Only to find the option IS NOT EVEN THERE.

So now, I have a watch that won’t pair to anything, empty icons on the screen with no smart functions at all.

I have loved Fossil for YEARS, decades really, and this is a really big let down. When you spend your hard earned money on a product, you expect it to work as advertised for more than a year. You definitely expect for the features to not change around on you each time fossil decides to tweak their apps.

title: Why would anyone design a backpack with a single center shaft?
text:
I really don't understand how anyone could possibly think that this is a good design. I considered buying this backpack and a similar one by Jeep, but quickly realized that it was guaranteed to break almost anything you could possibly carry in it other than clothing because of the single center bar.

To demonstrate the problem, put a pencil against the corner of your kitchen counter. Press on both ends of the pencil. What happens? The pencil breaks. The kitchen counter is acting as a fulcrum, the pencil as a lever with substantial force on both ends. In a similar fashion, when you put a book in this sort of backpack, everything else in the bag is pushing on both sides of that book, but the book is supported only in the center by that single vertical post. Unless you only carry light stuff in the bag, you're almost guaranteed to end up with bent notebooks, books with broken backs, laptops with cracked screens, and so on. It's quite possibly the worst backpack design mistake in the history of backpacks.

If you don't care about the stuff you're carrying, by all means, buy one of these. If what you are carrying has any value at all, do yourself a favor and buy something with a more traditional double handle or wait for the designers to come up with a single handle built into a solid back so that it doesn't turn your cargo into a lever with a heavy weight at both ends. There's not enough crack in the world for this design to make sense.

title: Does not stay secured
text:
For the most part, this was a pretty good project. It took a while to install the microphone and ear pieces in my helmet, but sounded great once I got it *just right*. Pairing with my android phone was simple. It was a little confusing at first that you press the side buttons to change the volume in "music" mode, but you have to turn the knob to change the volume in "phone" mode. I could hear just fine, but the person on the other end said I sounded kind of muffled when I tested at home.

For my first ride with it, I was using it to hear voice directions from my phone. When the directions were starting, I could hear some static, but the static stopped when the talking stopped. I didn't listen to music while I was riding because I wanted to keep my attention on the road, but it seemed to work fine testing at home. I didn't have anyone else to try the intercom with, and I didn't make or receive any phone calls while on the road. Unfortunately, that is the extent of my testing. When I got to the end of a long (8 mile) road, I noticed that my GPS wasn't giving me directions anymore. After I stopped, I realized that the bluetooth unit had fallen off somewhere in the last 8 miles! I only went a maximum of 45mph on that road and there weren't any hard turns or bumpy maneuvers. I followed the directions while installing... the clip-on attachment seemed a little loose so I used the stick-on one. The attachment stayed on the helmet, but the bluetooth part didn't stay on the attachment.

Based on my limited experience, I would probably rate it four stars, but unfortunately I can NOT recommend a product that may get lost the first time you use it. At this price, it is hardly disposable... I was very disappointed that it fell off but was unable to find it again despite my searching. The little tab that holds it in does not hold it tight. At the very least, it should have a loop or something so you can attach a safety cord to it in case it slips loose. Others may have better luck than I did, but I STRONGLY recommend using a second method to secure the device for anyone who is considering buying one- it is too expensive to lose it on your first use.

title: Total junk; cheaply made, fell apart within a month
text:
I bought one of these bags during a sale at our college bookstore. My faithful REI backpack, which lasted me nearly ten years, is finally developing zipper problems and so when I saw this bag for $20, I figured it was a good replacement for my current pack.

Unfortunately, this backpack went to pieces pretty quickly. In particular, the straps on both sides began to fray badly at the lower joint, within a month of purchase. I haven't done anything with this bag but carry books in it, but it's literally coming apart at the seams. My old REI pack, on the other hand, is still in pretty decent condition despite having been used on camping trips as well as for school.

On top of everything else, the bookstore refused to give me a refund since this was a clearance item... so I'm out twenty bucks and I need a new backpack again. Now I know why they had extras left over. Buyer beware.

title: Shame on Amazon for letting this crap go on
text: I do not own these, but have listened closely on a friend s pair. There are a number of reviews that are essentially paid for by receipt of product free. Many of these also have many people saying the review was helpful. I find that suspicious. I listen to a lot of music, all kinds, on a wide array of equipment. This includes phones from a couple thousand to earbuds. Before you clnk down some change, listen to a $250 or of phones, then compare several brands in this products price range. Tastes vary, but I think you'll see that these reviews are nothing more than advertising hype. Also, they don't give any specs. Do a comparison with similarly priced products. Taking these steps will give you a more valid eval than the paid for with product reviews. Shame on Amazon for letting this crap go on.

title: Does not charge reliably in many places in the world
text:
They claim “powered by body heat”, which it wil do some places, but not in warm climates.
So if you take a vacation to Mexico, it will stop charging.
When it stops charging, it will go dead, you will lose any data it has stored, and it will lose its pairing with your phone.
Support will then send you a “thermal charger” to get it back online again.
Thus, the claim that “it never needs charging” is false. This is not a reliable traveling watch. I am suprised they haven’t faced a lawsuit yet.
I really wanted this to be the watch I could rely on to survive for 20 years and forget about charging, but this is not that watch.
Additionall they have support and quality problems. It took them 4 months to send me a replacement watch band for the poor-quality one they ship with.

title: Do not buy, very poor quality
text: To start with, Garmin totally screwed up the 610 which is an otherwise fantastic with a proprietary band system that is horribly designed and incompatible with common watch bands. The original bands tear and fail making it very easy to lose your watch, and replacing the band is very difficult and often results in bending the pins or tearing the thin rubber where the pins are secured. This replacement band was even more frail than the original and tore instantly upon attempted installation making it unusable. Love the functionality and data collection, but with the band makes this Garmin and it’s strap system a total lemon.

title: Terrible Battery Life and Screen Glitch
text: I really wanted to love this watch. It's so pretty, gives me all my notifications, allows me to use google assistant, tracks my health, and works great with my Google Pixel. However, the battery life is seriously terrible. I start my work day at 8am and it's dead by noon. What good is a smart watch if it won't stay charged? I've done some research online and I guess you basically have to turn off all notifications but texts and calls, set the screen to practically dark, use the most boring watch face they offer (the cool animation ones take up a lot of battery life apparently) , and turn off the 'always on' feature to where you constantly have to turn your watch back on even see the time. Really disappointed. I figured a Google OS watch would be a lot better than that. The screen also glitches a lot and either won't do anything when you swipe or it will become pixelated and then appear. I would not recommend this watch. Returning it as soon as possible.

title: Almost a good watch, but very poor quality
text:
I have many Skagen watches, I love them because they are simple small, and for the most part, very well made for an inexpensive watch. For the $295 price tag, I would give one star. Luckily, Skagen has been selling these for $123 on their website recently, and I picked up one for that price. I'd give it a 2 to 3 star rating at that price.

Pros:
Very small and slim for a smart watch, that is what I love about Skagen design and why all my normal watches are mostly Skagen. I've had compliments on the watch when people find out it is a smart watch.
Battery life is good, it will last an entire work day.
GPS tracking plus heart rate monitoring makes it replace my Garmin watch.
Works very well with android for notifications, the save me the time to look at my phone.

Cons:
- Not at all water resistant. As an engineer of over 10 years, I can testify by looking at the seal when it failed, it is not water resistant. It was not developed by an engineer. The sad part is that after 30 min of applying a one-off proper seal, it worked, and can be applied in the production line in seconds.
- The magnetic strap is a cool idea, but has a lot of draw backs. If you work near anything magnetic or ferromagnetic, you have to remove the watch. If you use a Macbook Pro, you have to remove the watch to type, as the magnet either attaches to the magnets in the case that hold the lid closed, or it will trigger the lid closed switch and put your computer to sleep. I rather a standard watch band.
- Build quality is abysmal, the back of the watch is made of cheap plastic, and in less than a month of normal use, it popped off, as many have reported. The back will not simply snap back on as with all my other Skagen watches. The solution is to the glue it back on, but it will likely lose it's water resistance. (update, you need to apply a proper seal to fix it)
- The watch auto-updates itself. This is an issue because it updates the watch face software, the updated watch faces
play a tacky animation everytime you turn the screen on to check the time. This means you have to uninstall the updates everytime the watch auto-updates.

Overall for ~$100 it is a fun watch. You can replace the magnetic strap, fix the flawed back with glue, and uninstall updates when they occur. It is a nice device to act as a screen to show notifications from your phone, and track your miles and heart rate while running. For anything close to the retail price, I cannot recommend. It was soooo close to being great. Hopefully version 3 will have better software, a better watch band, and replace the plastic back with a standard metal or a better designed one. This watch doesn't use wireless charging, so having a metal back is absolutely possible.

title: This product is TOO small
text:
I bought this product as a gift, for my mother whose wrists are only slightly larger than mine. I have a Charge 2, and I wear that with a small band on the third smallest notch. I figured that this bracelet band would suit her just fine. WRONG! I thought we were going to have to break it off of her because it was too close to her skin to be worn comfortably, and the latches are so so so difficult to take apart. Once we finally got it off of her wrist, I thought that maybe I could use it (I'm planning on switching trackers soon). It was more comfortable on me to the point that I would wear it, but it was still too tight to slip my finger under to undo the clasp.
I am very disappointed with this product and it's page on Amazon. The product title and description are misleading, and unfortunately I only noticed this after receiving the product. This product, Silver Color-2, is small. Even when it's not worn, the clasps are incredibly difficult to unlatch. If you have an Alta HR or a Charge 2 (other models I'm sure have similar points of attachment), you know that the face can be disconnected from the band by somewhat flat metal clasps between both sides of the back of the face and the inside of the band. This bracelet band has a similar feature to attach to the face. Those pieces also attach to the smooth rounded part of the bracelet with other clasps that are nearly impossible to open. When it wasn't being worn, the clasps must be pushed in (towards the skin) a decent amount in order to be released.
I'll buy name brand next time.

title: Failed at literally the only thing it's made to do
text: I recently purchased this Faraday Bag because I am going through some legal issues with a previous employer who has resorted to hacking my devices to seize control of every device I own or buy in an effort to delete evidence.. I was at my wits end and thought maybe this bag would help keep them out of my computer and phones and bring a little peace into my life.  I certainly thought it was working at first based on the devices not responding to emails, calls, or texts while they're in the bag so I purchased a second one.  So when I bought a new laptop last night I felt somewhat safe with the belief that the compromised laptop wouldnt be able to connect to the new one as long as both werent out of their bags at the same time.  Wrong.  It only took literal seconds for the compromised laptop to communicate with the new one ... in it's Faraday Bag ...  down a short hall and a flight of stairs ...where it was resting inside of a closed metal mail box.  Seconds!  I watched the logs files of the new laptop being accessed in real time.  And now I have a second compromised laptop.  So I can't say I would recommend this if you need to protect your devices from anything more than a simple text or phone call

title: Used/packaged dirty/SMALL/very poorly designed/Not very loud /poor sound quality/ OVER PRICED
text:
The First thing I noticed out of box was how dirty it was, it looks as if it has been used :( .

The Second thing I noticed was the poor quality of this product , not just in quality alone but also in design as the location of the charge port is very difficult to get at, it is located on the inside of the ear muff and for what ever reason after charging it is difficult to disconnect the charger cord, the force I have to use to separate the cord from the charge port on the ear muff makes me afraid it is going to break while unplugging it.

Third thing i noticed was the first charge would not charge more then 10 min, after an hour and a half of playing it died, volume was at 80%.

Fourth thing I noticed was the sound quality was very disappointing, for the price you pay I did expect a little better sound quality, it is not much better then the sound quality from other cheaper devices I have used.
Fifth thing I noticed was the muff just fit, barely, but they do fit, the are not uncomfortable, but they are not comfortable either, they feel like they are going to fall off the whole time I wear them.

Over all I can say I paid to much for a dirty "I believe to be used" set of poorly designed ,cheaply made bluetooth winter earmuffs. They get the job done, they do keep your ears warm and you can listen to music wirelessly , however I will not purchase another pair for myself nor as a gift to anyone. I am not a buy and return person unless the product is broken or not as described , even though I believe them to be used do to how dirty they were , they are in working condition and they did not state they were new. :(

Just wanted to update my review, to add they they dont take a charge any more, it stopped taking a charge about three weeks ago, I switched out charging cables and tried different charges but nothing, just a very expensive uncomfortable headset that fails to keep my ears warm. :(

title: I wish I could give better [updated -- lower] ratings
text:
but there is often a very simple, fatal flaw.

See I wanted to glance at the watch to tell the time earlier today, something you'd expect to be able to do with a thing called a "watch." I turned over my wrist. It didn't work.

I'm typing to my colleagues on my work computer and I needed to know what time it was so I don't miss a meeting.

I turned over my wrist again.

Blank.

I turned over my wrist and shook it. (I imagined briefly how comical this would have looked in the meeting room.)

Finally. It flashed on the screen for a second or two and asked me to authenticate myself.

Authenticate? Authenticate?!

I just wanted to know what time it was. All of my other watches can do this just fine, and they are not the smart variety. Shouldn't a "smart" watch not be less useful?

Why should I need permission from my watch to get what time it was? Who is the owner here? Who serves whom? I don't need permission from my microwave to cook. I tell it a time and setting, and it just does it without trouble.

But the nerve of the "smart" watch!

I know authentication is necessary to protect private text messages and payment info, but to see the time and weather?! The thing is being an a-hole, which by transitive means the people who sold it intend to be a-holes to us users.

Fossil, I know you may not have written that bit of the software, but the bottom line is I bought the product from you. If you need to, yell at Google.

Update 1 --

I wish I can give a lower score than 1. Amazon, can we give negative scores? Or double zeros?

I bought a new phone. The phone would not pair with the new phone via bluetooth. But that isn't the way they want you to do it.

See. You have to on the watch, select "Factory Reset." Then on the phone open the Wear OS app, and then you get stuck at a later step. But I kid you not. That is the way they want you to do it, and it doesn't work.

I wish I can ask Fossil to refund my money, because this has cost me more in stress than any other piece of technology I have ever used, and I have used actual prototypes that are half-baked in the R&D labs.

title: Love the idea but this band fails to hold the Fitbit on my wrist
text: Love the idea of this watch band, but it just doesn’t work.  The magnet is exceptionally strong and one of the attachment points is weak.  As a result, anything from a hand clap or taking off my gloves to changing my hoodie or carrying out the trash causes the band to unlink from one side of the Fitbit and drop ... to the floor, sidewalk, trash can, wherever.  If it drops to the floor, I hear it.  If it falls on a carpet, the grass, or the trash can, I have to retrace my steps and look carefully for the fallen Fitbit.  I love the idea of this watch band and, when I sit quietly, it is very comfortable — so comfortable that I hardly feel it when I have it on (and I do not wear it loosely) and rarely notice when it has become de-linked and falls.  I have trained myself to feel my wrist regularly, and whenever I move my wrist, to see if it is still there.  My new watch bands, with a traditional buckles, arrive tomorrow — they won’t be as comfortable or as easy to wear but they’ll keep the Fitbit on my wrist.

title: The worst watch experience you could hope for...
text:
I have had more than one smart watch, So I had a realistic view of what to expect. This somehow managed to be so much lower than that I can't believe it.

YES the battery life is terrible. I can not get 9 hours out of it. This is with a static watch face (No moving animations) digital face, Ambient mode on Eco default and screen time out at 5 seconds. I have also erased everything off the watch except for 3 faces. That is all that I have downloaded to it.

The constant system error messages, The System memory warnings and the horrible battery life make this rather expensive watch behave and perform like a $29 Chinese knock off. My LG Urbane was by far the greatest watch I have owned. Never an error message and never a battery issue in 4 years. I miss that watch.

I have factory reset this three times over the last few months to try to remedy the issues it has and they continue to plague this watch.

This is a fashion accessory gone so bad I can't accurately explain it.

My experience is kind of my fault, I want to a fashion brand and purchased a piece of technology. I am moving on to Fossil who has been making smart watches for years and is trustworthy.

title: Total Bust, Super Disappointment
text:
*****8-31-19 UPDATE TO ORIGINAL REVIEW BELOW*****
We contacted Skagen customer service, and to their credit they were great. They said that the water intrusion is a known problem, and they promptly replaced the watch - and said to contact them again if the problem occurs again and they'll take care of it. The watch is still a 1 star item and not recommended, but Skagen was at least responsive.
****************************************************************
I bought a Samsung Gear S3 Frontier watch over two years ago, and I love it and have had zero problems with it. My wife wanted a smartwatch (not Apple as we are Android users) but didn't want something as big as the Samsung - so I got her this watch. I have two traditional Skagen watches I love, so I trusted the brand. The smartwatch is also smaller than some others, it looks good, and has a feminine style. I wasn't experienced with Google's Wear OS operating system, but assumed it would be good since it's a Google platform right? Wrong. I have been shocked by how bad it is from top to bottom.
Setting up the watch and pairing it with my wife's phone was easy - easy enough that she did it without my help, though I'm the techie and she doesn't typically have much patience for setting up devices. But what we noticed right off the bat was how slow and laggy the OS is. It consistently delays when you press the screen or side buttons, to the point of frustration and to the point that you sometimes wonder if it's broken or frozen. And several times it has frozen, with weird red lines across the screen and requiring a reboot to fix it. This is running Snapdragon's newest processor but you'd never know it, and the problem has to be Wear OS.
Not only is it slow, it also has absolutely terrible, completely unacceptable battery life. My two year old Samsung watch typically has about 60% battery charge left after a long day, and though I charge it every night it can last two days per charge. This one can't make it through a day at all, and that's doing everything you can to curb battery usage (having the screen auto-sleep when you're not using it, disabling features, setting the brightness very low, etc.). I wondered if we were doing something wrong so I searched the web and found that the terrible battery life is common to Wear OS in general and certainly common to this watch. I am amazed that Google hasn't optimized their OS better than this. So if you get this watch, plan to charge it several times per day to keep it going.
On top of the bad performance and battery, the watch is also poorly constructed. It's supposed to be waterproof, but after just 3 weeks of having it and wearing it in the pool a couple of times, my wife's watch had water get into the body and it died. We had never opened the watch, messed with it, or done anything with it outside of normal wear and usage to explain why or how the water got in. Now it's a paper weight.
What a shame. My wife loved the look of the watch and it has the right fitness and smart features (voice control, messaging, etc.). But between the software bugs, abysmal battery life, and structural failure, this Skagen was a total bust and super disappointment. Avoid it.

text: It looks great, exactly what I expected and what I wanted, but the SMELL is unbearable, an absolute stinkfest. It's as if they used "natural" methods to cure the leather, like soaking in animal urine, then didn't fully rinse it afterward.  For the past week I've been airing it out and spraying it with Febreeze, to no avail. I've now locked it in a box with some baking soda and a strong odor absorbing gel I got at Bed Bath and Beyond, and will let it ripen for a while. If that does not leach out the smell, I'm not sure what to do, but for right now, my money was wasted and flushed down the toilet.  If I can eliminate the odor, I will be quite pleased.  Perhaps in the dry, hot air of India where it is made, the leather smells are not emitted and it smells good; once in the humid Maryland air all the toxins come leaching out to pollute the nose.  I'd be interested to see if others have the same odor problem, and what they did to get rid of it.

title: Hmm, Good, but!
text:
Edit after couple days of use: I had to replace the shoulder strap to hang it in the center, this was about additional $15 to the already bag's heigh cost.
Also, notice, the received bag is not contain same additional sides as the one in the add.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------
So, I got this bag as a present from my wife, however, already I have Flight Gear for more than a year and a half so far (in the photos), and while moving items between bags, I decided to write a comparison between both.
Long story short, the Flight Gear for me is better than Flex B4.

Why? Here is why:

Generally, Flex B4 have similar dimensions to Flight Gear, or little bigger, but the extra little size will not be wisely utilized as we will see later in this review.
Flex B4 is noticeably heavier than Flight Gear,
Flex B4 have Either the handle or the shoulder strap must NOT be in the center of bag's gravity, means it will will not be comfortable when carrying.
Believe it or not, Flex B4 will carry exact same items as the Flight Gear, in fact, I believe Flex B4 will carry less than Flight Gear.
Don't get fooled by the top opening for the headset in Flex B4, the handle is in the way when taking the headset out, at some point, you will break the headset's cable.
As you see, in the Flex B4, I had to remove the side bottle holder to put hand held lights instead.
Flex B4 is bulky compared to the right well measured Flight Gear.
Yes, Flex B4 have lot of front compartments, but they are all stacked on top of each other (I counted 10 of them in that one front side) making it kind of unusable unless you are carrying small coins or paper tags.
Oh, Flex B4 have lot of zippers (a lot), main ones are bulky plastic to keep the bag together, believe me, they are important, compared to the Flight Gear it's all sewed to one peice and zippers that you may not care if it fail (none fail so far in the Flight Gear)
And as we see the price, Flex B4 is almost triple priced

I will start using Flex B4 because my wife bought it to me, , but, Honestly, I will not recommend it to another pilot.

So what's good in it?

Hmm, just showing you can spend so much money!
It have glasses place that you may like, but I use it for pens because Flex B4 have no reliable pens/pencil holders.
Also, Flex B4 is expandable as you see, however, this will make it heavier and more unstable because the carrying handl/shoulder strap will not be in the center.
Oh, it have a luggage handle holder, but not sure how important it is though.

If the manufacturer will listen, then I would suggest adding some colors to make it happier, adding some straps to hang some lights, and some places for pens as well.
Also, some easy access to the modern life of tablets and iPads.

But, this will be me, not the manufacturer.

title: It's a mission impossible watch band
text: It self-destructs in 2 days! It was received on a Friday and by Sunday morning it was broken. I bought this as a gift and am embarrassed over picking something so poor quality. I will be returning it. Stay away.

title: You get what you pay for.
text:
Upsides:
-Large Capacity. This will hold my Alienware 17R4 gaming laptop, massive power brick, mouse, mouspad, 5 fan laptop cooling pad and all of my paperwork for work.
-Sturdy leather. This bag has been handling 15 pounds daily for months and months with no signs of impending failure.
-Multiple internal compartments to separate lap top from accessories and from my work documents that could easily be damaged if they were in a common storage area.
-Professional appearance. Unlike lugging around an Alienware specific shoulder bag, I can conceal that I am packing a huge gaming laptop.
-Price. Even for its downsides, the bag is still a good value.

Downsides:
-It only comes in brown. While some may like brown, they should still offer it in black since that is the most popular color in the working world. Brown actually draws attention because hardly anyone carries a brown bag.
-This bag uses centuries old metal buckle designs. While they look cool, using them is not fun. Most modern bags have buckles that look old school, but function 5x faster and with less hassle because they click to lock, rather than undoing each strap like it’s a belt with belt buckle.
-The hardware is cheap. Too cheap. This is the biggest downside to this product and it is one that they could have cured by using hardware that was $5 more. All of the pieces are thin, stamped or rolled steel. The Tongues on the buckle are so thin, they are almost sharp. The rollers on the buckles are so thin that they rattle around with a high-pitched , tinny sound. Most nice bags use thick pot metal or cast iron that is finished by hand to look rustic or thicker steel that is perfectly finished by a machine. This bag has thin steel parts that are finished by hand and it gives it a cheap “made in the third world” feel to it.
-They advertise this as a computer bag, but the interior pockets are made out of very thick, very rough canvas. This is very sturdy, but not friendly to your lap top’s case, how soft nylon or fleece is.
-No internal padding to protect your laptop.
-The center pocket is zippered and the metal zipper clasp will drag across your laptop’s case every time you take it out or put it away.
-The leather straps to close the bag are too long. Loaded with lap top, accessories and work paperwork, I put the main flap’s trap to the tightest setting and it is still not tight. If you have to use emergency braking in your car and the bag takes a tumble, for certain, some of your stuff will come flying out.
-The shoulder strap is too long. I am 6’3” and with the shoulder strap to the shortest setting, the bag still hits me mid thigh, which is painful when you are packing 15 lbs in it. It needs to firmly rest on your hip/buttocks and no lower. This happens with same shoulder carry, or opposite shoulder carry.

Conclusion:
This bag is good, but it is nowhere near perfect. If I had to buy a new one, I would want to find one that is this size and durability, but in black and with better clasps/hardware and with internal padding. With that said, I haven’t found a bag like that and I might end up buying this bag again.

Update 10/19/18: After 10.5 months, 2 out of 6 of the hand strap rivets have failed by separating the front and rear components of the rivet. Also, 1 of 4 shoulder strap rivets have failed. As the rivets failed, so did the stitching. Both the hand strap and shoulder strap are now at risk of failing and I am shopping for a replacement. See attached photos.

title: Pretty but useless
text:
In the box, this is really pretty. I will give it that. Once I opened the box, all hell broke loose. First, it was a real pain to install. No quick release pins means I fought the pins with the micro-fork tool thingy and chipped a few nails. But I did get it installed, finally. Within 2 days it seemed cheesy and plastic looking, so I took it off. Then I noticed the holes that hold the pins to connect to the watch had chipped. You can see the chips in the top left and bottom right. That's where you fight with the pins to insert them into the watch frame. Total disappointment. I switched to one of the magnetic mesh bands and like it much better.
Pros: lightweight, pretty, arrived quickly
Cons: very difficult to install, totally plastic, broke where material is too weak and thin

title: Quality and Customer Service Issues
text: I purchased a Roller Derby a few weeks ago. The quality of the bag is on the high side as expected according to the price point. However, the bag started squeaking near the retractable handle to an unacceptable level and so I contacted Think Tank for a replacement. I was even ready to send it in for repair. The response to my inquiry was almost laughable even though I appreciated that the product manager wrote a long message back to me saying how sorry he is on behalf of the company. What I received is an instruction with all the details and pictures showing what tools I need and etc for self-repair. Yes, I was basically asked to fix it myself. The suggested fix actually requires a complete disassembly of the retractable handle from the insdie of the bag. If I decide to fix it myself, I will have to go to a hardware store to buy the tools first. I do not think it is appropriate for a product manager to tell an unhappy customer to do a self-repair. In my opinion, the first step is to offer warranty repairs since the bag is only a few weeks old. I am utterly unhappy with the bag and know that the bag is the last thing I will buy from Think Tank.

title: Are they paranoid with illegal copying?
text:
You cannot install to base camp. You can view it on base camp only when your GPS is plugged in to the pc with the hunt chip installed into the GPS or remove the chip from the GPS and place in to a SD reader and plug that into the PC. Older versions had instructions where you could install the hunt chip into base camp and leave the GPS with the hunt chip into it at the cabin. The chip is only 4GB in size. Much to small to install additional maps you may want to have installed such as openstreet or other Topo maps. No ability to install the hunt map onto a larger SD chip is given. This means you are contentiously removing and installing SD chips from your GPS. And if you do install additional map IMG files into the small area remaining, one mistake and you could destroy the Hunt data on the chip since there is no way to backup the hunt SD chip data to your pc.

The map is CLUTTERED with all the hunt area information that one does NOT need approximately 11 months a year with no option to hide hunt area data. It would be much better if you could hide different layers such as Antelope and Deer of you wanted Elk data only. Land ownership data and the hunt area data is quickly outdated. yearly updates are an additional $30 a year.

title: Terrible User Interface
text:
What I don't like
- The watch face is in dim, poorly contrasting colors which are hard to see. It makes me feel like I'm color blind and I have to turn on the back light in order to read the watch. I've called their tech support and they put in a request for me for new "faces" that would take care of it, but who knows when that might happen?
- It takes at at least 7 clicks to get to the stop watch function and back to the main screen.
- You can't see previous day's steps on the watch. You can only see them in the phone app.
- You can only track heart rate history through work outs. If you don't use work outs, then you can't track your heart rate.
- Tech support had to keep going back to the experts to answer my support questions. They had little direct knowledge of the product. When I asked what was the design mechanism for tracking steps when GPS is off, the support person had to go ask and came back with "it's some sort of pedometer". That was a non answer.
- The users manual does not come with the product. You have to download it as a PDF. It's very sparse, not well illustrated, and difficult to make sense of. I had to end up calling tech support, just to get the watch working. It doesn't work out of the box. It doesn't even do local functions until you connect to the internet via bluetooth and download the operating system.
- There are many more terrible things about the user interface - too many to describe

What I do like
- I love that the watch keeps my daily steps. I've used pedometers all my waking hours for 25 years and they always break down, especially when I sweat in work outs. Now, I've finally stopped using pedometers.
- I've tested the accuracy of the step counts a couple times and it seems to be on target
- I love having a step counter that I don't have to wear separately that chafes my skin, dies when it gets wet, or is dropped, etc.
- I like that it tracks my sleep patterns and even gives deep sleep, light sleep, and awake in bed figures, although I don't trust them fully.
- I like how seamlessly it connects to the phone app and automatically uploads from the watch to the app
- I like how when it connects, it automatically downloads the latest operating system updates

title: lol no (updated as of 7/24)
text:
7/24 update:
I settled on Withings Fitness watch here: https://www.amazon.com/Withings-Nokia-Steel-Sport-Smartwatch/dp/B07GXR9V19
Since buying it on May 26th for $140, I couldn't be happier.

Original review:
Returning this watch after 1 day because it's not a fitness watch. This is just a runners watch, at best.

As of the date of this review:
- The watch provides NO data to Google fit or Strava, you can simply link your account to them, but ZERO data is fed to those apps.
- The watch tracks your heart rate and O2 levels in detail, ONLY when you are running.
- You can leave the heart rate sensor on, but since nothing is fed to the gshockmove app or Google fit, no stats are recorded, you get to see your heart rate on your wrist, that's it.
- As I said before, the watch records data when you are RUNNING. If you weight train, swim, do yoga, aerobics or play any sport, forget this watch because all you'll get is a GPS map showing you walking around the gym or whatever your physical positions were on the field if you played ball.

I contacted Casio tech support and asked if anyone there could help me get this watch to record any data aside from if I run, the guy put me on hold while he did whatever, and then basically told me NO.

There are watches that cost a FRACTION of this one that do more besides recording my running lap times, and also, if you were a running enthusiast, why would you even get this watch? It's big, heavy, and bulky.

Bottom line:
- no data fed to Google fit
- no data recorded aside from if/when you run and actually physically press the 'run' button on the watch
- you are paying $400 for a watch that doesn't do the basic functions that $50 watches do

Final thoughts: this watch has the look, it has the hardware, but if no data is recorded or fed to 3rd party apps, this is just another item from the bugman/redditor starter kit.

Sorry Casio, I don't know who you think is buying this watch, but we are not all professional runners timing our laps to the water cooler.

title: Comfy and sounds good
text:
UPDATE: 8.23.2019 I knocked these down to one star because the audio no longer works at all. I know it is these headphones because I tried a different pair and the other pair worked fine. For how short a time I had them (compared to cheaper pairs that lasted a lot longer) these were not worth the money to me.

I bought these over two years ago, and I still love them. I wear eyeglasses, and these headphones don't squeeze the temple tips of the frames into my head, so I can wear these headphones comfortably for extended periods of listening. They are also easy to adjust, and hold adjustments well.
These are primarily used for audiobook listening and they have been excellent. I'm no audio connoisseur, so I can't give a detailed assessment about the sound quality for listening to music. All I can tell you is that what I hear sounds great.
My only grievance is how twisted the cord can get and how often I have to untwist it (once a week). Since the cord twisting is such a minor thing I wasn't going to knock off a star for it.
